text: Dogs should be bathed every 4-6 weeks. Over-bathing strips natural oils and causes dry, itchy skin.
Cats groom themselves but benefit from weekly brushing to reduce shedding and hairballs.
Puppies need core vaccines (DHPP) at 8, 12, and 16 weeks, then a booster at 1 year.
Adult dogs need annual DHPP and rabies boosters. Ask your vet about Bordetella if your dog visits parks or daycares.
Cats need FVRCP and rabies vaccines. Indoor cats still require rabies vaccination by law in most states.
Adult dogs should eat 2 meals per day. Puppies under 6 months need 3 smaller meals per day.
Cats are obligate carnivores and require taurine in their diet. Never feed a cat dog food as their primary diet.
Most adult dogs need 30-60 minutes of exercise per day. High-energy breeds like Border Collies or Huskies need 2+ hours.
Small dog breeds like Chihuahuas and Pugs need only 20-30 minutes of light exercise per day.
Signs a dog needs grooming: matted fur, nails clicking on the floor, watery eyes from overgrown face fur.
Flea and tick prevention should be applied monthly year-round. Check your pet after outdoor time in wooded or grassy areas.
Cats should have one litter box per cat, plus one extra. Scoop daily and do a full clean with soap weekly.
Senior dogs (7+ years for large breeds, 10+ for small) should visit the vet every 6 months for early detection of arthritis or organ issues.
Senior cats (11+ years) benefit from twice-yearly vet visits and may need kidney or thyroid screenings.
Dogs should not eat chocolate, grapes, raisins, onions, garlic, xylitol, or avocado — all are toxic.
Cats should not eat onions, garlic, raw dough, alcohol, or lilies. Lilies are especially dangerous and can cause kidney failure.
Dental health matters: brush your dog's teeth 2-3 times per week. Dental chews can help but do not replace brushing.
Cats rarely drink enough water. Wet food or a pet water fountain can help prevent urinary tract problems.
Dogs should have access to fresh water at all times, especially after exercise or in hot weather.
Heartworm prevention medication should be given monthly. Dogs should be tested for heartworm annually before starting prevention.
Spaying or neutering is recommended around 6 months for most cats and small dogs. Larger dog breeds may benefit from waiting until 12-18 months.
A healthy dog's nose can be wet or dry — neither is a reliable sign of illness on its own. Watch for discharge or cracking instead.
Normal dog body temperature is 101-102.5°F. Anything above 104°F is a fever requiring vet attention.
Normal cat body temperature is 100.5-102.5°F. Lethargy, hiding, or loss of appetite are common signs something is wrong.
Cats should be kept indoors or supervised outdoors. Outdoor cats have significantly shorter lifespans due to cars, predators, and disease.
Dogs are social animals and can develop separation anxiety if left alone too long. Crate training and enrichment toys can help.
Interactive toys and puzzle feeders help prevent boredom in both dogs and cats, especially when left alone.
Rabbit diet should be 70% hay, with fresh leafy greens and limited pellets. Avoid sugary fruits and starchy vegetables.
Guinea pigs cannot produce their own vitamin C and must get it through fresh vegetables like bell peppers and leafy greens.
Birds need daily social interaction and mental stimulation. A bored parrot can develop feather-plucking behavior.
